Title: Ghosts of Consumption (for Piet M.)
Creator: Pam Longobardi
Material: Found ocean plastic from Hawaii, Alaska, Greece, Costa Rica, Italy, and the Gulf of Mexico
Location: Crystal Bridges Museum of American Art
Description: A sculptural installation by Pam Longobardi that addresses plastic pollution, global consumption, and environmental loss. Constructed from discarded plastic debris collected from oceans and shorelines, the work assembles colorful fragments into a chaotic yet deliberate composition. The piece evokes the lingering “ghosts” of consumer culture—objects designed for convenience that outlive their usefulness and return as environmental threats. By transforming waste into art, Longobardi reveals how plastics migrate across borders and ecosystems, implicating global systems of production and consumption.

Title: Toxic Evolution
Creator: Alvaro Soler-Arpa
Material: Different animal bones, wire and plastic waste
Description: The works imagine a near future in which animal life has been radically altered by human-made pollution and genetic contamination. Presented through a false scientific format, the work depicts elegant yet disturbing hybrid creatures whose mutations—caused by toxicity, transgenics, and environmental waste—challenge the natural rhythms of evolution and geological time. These fictitious beings symbolize the consequences of humanity’s irresponsible impact on nature.

Title: Bovine Divine
Creator: Siddhartha Kararwal
Description: The 4-pound Styrofoam cow was created to hghlight the plight of abandoned cows left to die on the streets. The animals considered holy often succumbed after consuming plastic from garbage dumps. The work was taken down after being deemed religiously offensive..

Title: Creatives Against #CAA
Creator: Kadak Collective
Description: An online archive containing various creative and informational material, including printable files,protest posters, zines, images, and  infographics explaining the Citizenship (Amendment) Act, 2019 (CAA) and the National Register of Citizens (NRC).The CAA act along with NRC was critiqued as an attempt to deny citizenship based on religion and disenfranchise Indian Muslims.

Title: Anti-CAA Murals
Creator: Simeen Anjum
Description: Simeen Anjum, a fine art student at Jamia Millia Islamia and other artists came to represent the artistic expression of political dissent through the creation of murals and graffiti in public spaces. The anti-CAA and NRC movement mobilized the public at the grassroots level and art functioned as a powerful tool for resistance.

Title: Shackville
Creator: Rhodes Must Fall Movement
Description: A protest installation constructed in 2016 by Rhodes Must Fall student activists at the University of Cape Town (UCT) to draw attention to severe campus housing shortages and unequal living conditions. The structure was a makeshift shack built from corrugated metal, wood, and other scavenged materials, deliberately resembling informal housing found in South African townships. Placed prominently on campus, the shack functioned as both a symbol and an intervention, confronting the university community with the material realities faced by many Black and working-class students who were excluded from adequate accommodation. By inserting an informal dwelling into an elite academic space, the protest highlighted the persistence of structural inequality, spatial injustice, and the unfinished business of decolonization in post-apartheid South Africa.

Title: The Son of a Migrant from Syria
Creator: Bansky
Description: A mural by Banksy painted in Calais, France, near the “Jungle” refugee camp that foregrounds Apple founder Steve Jobs’s immigrant background and significant contributions to his host country, positioning the work as a critique of the escalating anti-immigrant rhetoric across Europe and the United States. The work reframes migrants as innovators, contributors and figures of potential and humanity,

Title: Love Is In The Air, Flower Thrower
Creator: Bansky
Description: This is a 2003 mural by Banksy, originally painted in Jerusalem. The image depicts a masked protester, dressed like a rioter and caught mid-throw, but instead of a weapon he hurls a bouquet of brightly colored flowers. The work juxtaposes violence and aggression against peace and hope.

Title: Slave Labor
Creator: Bansky
Description: Also known as Bunting Boy, the 2012 mural by the anonymous street artist Banksy, was originally painted on the side of a shop in Wood Green, London. The work shows a young child hunched over a sewing machine, producing a string of Union Jack bunting. The image is a sharp critique of exploitative labor practices, particularly child labor and sweatshop production linked to Western consumer culture.

Title: Do Women still have to be naked to get into the MET Museum
Creator: Guerilla Girls
Description: The Guerrilla Girls are an anonymous feminist activist art collective founded in 1985 in New York. Their work exposes sexism, racism, and power imbalances in the art world and broader culture, especially within museums, galleries, Hollywood, and politics. They are best known for bold posters, billboards, and public interventions that combine statistics, sharp humor, and graphic design. Using a visual style influenced by advertising — high-contrast colors, large type, and punchy slogans — they present hard data about the underrepresentation of women and artists of color. One of their most famous posters asks: “Do women have to be naked to get into the Met. Museum?” pairing the question with a statistic about how few women artists are shown compared to how many nude female bodies appear in the collection. The collective members wear gorilla masks and adopt the names of deceased women artists to maintain anonymity. This shifts attention away from individual authorship and toward systemic critique, while also protecting members from professional backlash

Title: Q: And Babies? A: And Babies
Creator: Irving Petlin, Jon Hendricks, Frazier Dougherty, The Artists' Poster Committee of the Art Workers' Coalition, Original photograph of the My Lai massacre by R. L. Haeberle
Description: The work is an anti-Vietnam war poster created by members of the Poster Committee of the New York–based Art Workers Coalition (AWC). The poster pairs a photograph of the 1968 My Lai massacre with the blunt Q&A confirmation from a U.S. Army officer that children were killed by soldiers in the massacre. The work forces viewers to confront the reality of U.S. military violence and the moral consequences of the war. The poster was widely distributed and became one of the most iconic examples of political art and activist graphic design of the Vietnam War era

Title: Metalwork, Mining the Museum
Creator: Fred Wilson
Location: Maryland Center for History and Culture
Description: Mining the Museum is a groundbreaking installation by American conceptual artist Fred Wilson that was first shown at the Maryland Historical Society in Baltimore from April 4, 1992, to February 28, 1993. Wilson utilized the collection to intervene in their conventional display and confront institutional narratives around history, culture and race—particularly the histories of African Americans and Native Americans.

Title: Untitled (Your body is a battleground)
Creator: Barbara Kruger
Description: Barbara Kruger created one of the most iconic feminist artworks for the 1989 women's march on Washington in support of reproductive rights. This was in response to the political efforts that threatened the landmark U.S. Supreme court decision protecting bodily autonomy.

Title: SILENCE=DEATH
Creator: Avram Finkelstein, Brian Howard, Oliver Johnston, Charles Kreloff, Chris Li
Description: In 1987, artists Avram Finkelstein, Brian Howard, Oliver Johnston, Charles Kreloff, Chris Lione, and Jorge Socarrás created the iconic SILENCE=DEATH featuring the pink symbol referencing Nazi persecution of LGBTQ. The symbol was adopted by the direct action advocacy group ACT UP in their fight against the AIDS pandemic,

Title: Kissing Doesn’t Kill: Greed and Indifference Do
Creator: Gran Fury
Description: Gran Fury, a New York-based AIDS activist artist collective (1988–1994) that emerged from ACT UP created this landmark artwork employed in bold 1980s visual campaigns. The famous image of diverse couples kissing, confronts homophobia, racism, government inaction, and corporate greed during the HIV/AIDS crisis, reframing intimacy as harmless while exposing indifference and profit-driven policies as deadly. The image was dispersed through postcards and posters featured on public buses running in major US cities.

Title: National Covid Memorial Wall
Creator: Friends of the Wall
Description: A public memorial along the River Thames in London, made up of hundreds of thousands of hand-painted hearts—each representing a life lost to COVID-19 in the UK. Created by bereaved families and volunteers, it serves as a place of remembrance, collective mourning, while also representing a grassroots response to government mismanagement of the crisis that led to preventable deaths.

Title: George Floyd and the Anti-Racist Archive
Creator: Anonymous
Description: Urban Art Mapping is a collaborative project across disciplines that archives street art in various forms such as graffitti, tags, stickers, throw-ups, wheat pastes, murals, and projections. Their collections include works supporting the racial justice movement following George Floyd's killing.

Title: Youth Activist Art Archive (YAAA)
Creator: YAAA artists
Visualtags: George Floyd, BLM, immigration, LGBTQIA, environment, racial injustice, gender inequality
Description: YAAA is a growing art collection created by young artists that addresses a range of social issues, from racial injustice to immigration. Artists aged 26 and younger may submit artwork for inclusion in the archive. Submissions are reviewed and curated by the YAAA team (students and faculty) at the University of Utah.

Title: United States of Attica
Creator: Faith Ringgold
Description: A politcal poster is the artist's response to the police action killing 39 people at the Attica Correctional facility when inmates took hostages to protest prison conditions. The poster features a map rendered in the pan-African flag colors with handwritten entires of other American atrocities including  anti-Chinese riots in Oregon in the 1880s, the Trail of Tears in Oklahoma that killed some 4,000 Cherokee Indians, the lynching of Emmett Till in Mississippi, the 1968 assassination of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. in Tennessee, riots in California, Michigan and Texas.

Title: Robert E. Lee Statue
Creator: Dustin Klein, Alex Criqui
Description: The artists recontextulaized the conferederate monument by projecting images of Black people killed by police,  including Floyd, Breonna Taylor and Marcus-David Peters, historical figures who fought for equality, such as Harriet Tubman, the Rev. Martin Luther King Jr., Frederick Douglass etc on the pedestal of the Robert E. Lee statue.

Title: A Man was Lynched by Police Yesterday
Creator: Dread Scott
Description: In the 1920s-30s, the day after someone was lynched the NAACP flew the iconic flag 'A Man Was Lynched Yesterday' outsider their national headquarters in NYC. Dread Scott created an updated version of the flag in response to the police killing of Walter Scott in 2015 and the flag has since been displayed at several locations including on the facade of Jack Shainman Gallery in New York during a 2016 exhibition.

Title: On the Impossibility of Freedom in a Country Founded on Slavery and Genocide
Creator: Dread Scott
Description: In the performance, Dread Scott attempted to work forward while being repeatedly targetted by the direct force of a fire hose referencing the history of violent government action against peaceful protestors. The performance stills show Scott in the iconic hands up don't shoot gesture pointing to the ongoing struggle for racial justice.

Title: Priya's Shakti
Creator: Ram Devineni, Dan Goldman, Vikas K. Menon, Lina Srivastava
Description: A graphic novel and multimedia comic project co-created by Ram Devineni that reimagines a sexual violence survivor as a superhero to confront gender-based violence and societal stigma. The comic is inspired by mytholog, a choice meant to reach the masses through a relatable medium. Apart from the digital/ hardcopy comic version, the project also exists in the form of murals in Dharavi, Asia's largest slum based in Mumbai, India.

Title: KinAct
Creator: Kinshasa collective
Description: Groups of performers and artists in the Democratic Republic of the Congo’s capital who transform trash and discarded materials into striking wearable art, costumes and performances to comment on environmental, political and social issues.

Title: Faces and Phases
Creator: Zanele Muholi
Description: An ongoing photographic portrait project the Johannesburg-based visual activist and artist began in 2006 to document, honor, and amplify the lives of Black lesbian, bisexual, Queer, transgender, and gender-nonconforming communities, particularly in South Africa. Muholi created Faces and Phases as a visual archive and act of activism, countering historical invisibility and marginalization by giving these communities visibility, dignity, and voice.

Title: Maboneng Township Arts Experience
Creator: Palesa Ngwenya, Siphiwe Ngwenya
Description: A nonprofit arts and cultural initiative that transforms township communities into creative and tourism destinations by turning homes into art galleries and streets into performance and exhibition spaces. It offers guided tours and immersive experiences—both in person and virtual—where visitors can meet local artists, explore exhibitions in gallery homes, participate in workshops, and learn about township heritage and contemporary culture. The project supports local artists and families by generating income, building skills, and reshaping perceptions of townships as vibrant cultural spaces rather than marginalized areas.

Title: Shirin Neshat: Born of Fire
Creator: Shrin Neshat
Description: A major solo exhibition by Iranian-born, New York–based artist and filmmaker Shirin Neshat, held at the Parrish Art Museum in 2025. The show brought together four key bodies of work that explore themes such as female empowerment, political resistance, exile and displacementand the cultural tensions between East and West. Included was the Women of Allah series (1993–7)—images inspired by women’s involvement in the Islamic Revolution and Iran-Iraq War.

Title: Bottled Ocean 2122
Creator: George Nuku
Description: A large-scale immersive art installation by the New Zealand-born Māori artist that imagines what the world’s oceans might look like 100 years in the future if plastic pollution continues unchecked. It transforms the interior of Vienna’s historic Theseus Temple into a surreal “underwater” environment made almost entirely of discarded plastics — especially empty bottles and styrofoam — reimagined as sea creatures, coral reefs, deities and marine landscapes. The work surrounds visitors with mutated forms of rays, sharks, fish and jellyfish crafted from plastic waste and places them in a sunken temple setting lined with deities representing elements like water, air and sea. What first appears beautiful and mesmerizing reveals itself as a dystopian vision of an ocean where marine life and environment have been overtaken by plastic, prompting reflection on humanity’s relationship with single-use materials.

Title: The Connecting Hearts Project
Creator: Penny Ryan
Description: A socially engaged art initiative that ran from 2016 to 2019 and invited thousands of people across cities like Sydney, London, Canberra and Adelaide to take part in making small terracotta human hearts in workshops. Participants from diverse backgrounds shaped and inscribed their hearts with personal reflections on themes like belonging, human connection and migration — particularly focusing on people seeking asylum and how we relate to one another across borders. The clay hearts were then installed in large public arrangements, often laid out in spirals that visitors could walk through, pick up, unwrap and add their own messages, creating a collective artwork about shared humanity and connection across identity and geographic boundaries.

Title: Made in Hong Kong, Banned in China
Creator: Badiucao
Description: A politically charged art exhibition by Chinese-Australian artist Badiucao that had its world premiere in Melbourne after it was cancelled in Hong Kong due to reported threats from Chinese authorities. Show included bold satirical cartoons, portraits of pro-democracy figures, symbolic neon pieces and imagery critiquing censorship, human rights abuses and political repression under the Chinese Communist Party. The exhibition uses visual metaphors and street art techniques to confront issues around freedom of expression, political power and the fraught political situation in Hong Kong.

Title: Snake Ceiling
Creator: Ai Weiwei
Description: A large-scale installation in which the artist suspended hundreds of children’s backpacks from a gallery ceiling in a long, twisting, snake-like form. The work was created in response to the 2008 Sichuan earthquake, in which thousands of poorly constructed school buildings collapsed and many students died; the backpacks symbolize the lost children and act as a poignant memorial and critique of the disaster and the government’s mishandling of the tragedy.

Title: Mural of the Lebanese singer and actress Sabah
Creator: Yazan Halwani
Description: Large, visually striking street-art works that blend Arabic calligraphy with figurative portraiture, transforming urban walls into cultural narratives rather than political messages. He paints oversized portraits of beloved Lebanese and Arab figures — such as singers Fairouz and Sabah, poets like Khalil Gibran, and everyday local personalities — using stylized calligraphic shapes as the building blocks of his images.His work celebrates shared heritage and identity, countering the city’s history of sectarian imagery and fostering a positive cultural memory.

Title: Don’t leave the cage unless strictly necessary
Creator: Beijing Film Academy student
Description: A performance art piece by a student from the Beijing Film Academy went viral on Chinese social media — before being quickly censored — as a form of protest against strict COVID-19 lockdown measures on campus. In the piece, the student sat inside a small cage, blindfolded with a surgical mask, with a sign above him that read: “Don’t leave the cage unless strictly necessary.” The phrase parodies the school’s official rule, “Don’t leave campus unless strictly necessary,” and was widely interpreted as a critique of the indefinite, heavy-handed lockdown, reflecting students’ frustration with limited freedom and opaque approval processes to go outside.

Title: The Happiness of Obedience
Creator: Guo Yingguang
Description: A performative documentary work created by the artist in 2015 after encountering the Shanghai marriage market — a public matchmaking scene where parents advertise their adult children to find spouses. As part of the project, Guo, then in her early 30s and unmarried (a status socially labeled in China as “left-over woman”), placed an “advertisement” for herself seeking a partner and documented the reactions of parents and others around her with photos and video. The resulting images and footage capture the social pressures, judgments, and awkward encounters tied to expectations around marriage and conformity in contemporary Chinese society.

Title: Les Femmes du Maroc
Creator: Lalla Essaydi
Description: A photographic series in which the artist reclaims and reinterprets historical depictions of Arab women — especially 19th-century Orientalist paintings — from her own female, North African perspective. In these large-scale, carefully staged images, Moroccan women are posed in compositions drawn from classical Western art but clothed, adorned, and embedded with layers of Arabic calligraphy applied in henna, a traditionally feminine practice. The work subverts exoticized, sexualized Western fantasies of veiled women and harems by replacing them with nuanced portraits that foreground identity, interiority, agency, and cultural complexity, challenging both colonial and patriarchal representations.

Title: Statue of Peace
Creator: Kim Seo-kyung, Kim Eun-sung
Description: A bronze memorial sculpture installed on December 14, 2011, opposite the Japanese embassy. It depicts a young Korean girl in traditional dress sitting on a chair and looking toward the embassy. The statue was created to honor and remember the victims of Japanese military sexual slavery known as “comfort women” during World War II and to call for acknowledgment and apology from the Japanese government. An empty chair beside her symbolizes both the loss of victims who have died and an invitation for observers to reflect on their suffering. In August 2017, several of Seoul’s city buses—particularly on Route 151—were temporarily fitted with “comfort women” statues placed on seats inside the buses as a mobile memorial to the wartime victims.

Title: Marry the Blue Sky
Creator: Kong Ning
Description: A piece of environmental performance art by Chinese artist Kong Ning in which she symbolically “marries” the sky to call attention to air pollution and the loss of clean air. In the performance—often staged in smog-affected Beijing—she wears an elaborate wedding dress made from hundreds of anti-pollution face masks as she walks through public spaces or poses in front of landmarks like the CCTV headquarters, visually dramatizing the contrast between a bride and the polluted sky above. The marriage metaphor underscores a poignant commitment to environmental protection and highlights the impacts of smog on daily life. The work went viral and has been widely cited as artistic activism around China’s air quality debate.

Title: It's A Matter Of Trust
Creator: Sam Humphreys
Description: An art-photography series in which the artist uses Barbi dolls as a powerful visual metaphor to confront the often hidden reality of domestic abuse and violence against women. Rather than showing the toy in its usual perfect, glamorous form, Humphreys alters the dolls with bruises, black eyes, blood, and other signs of trauma to illustrate how idealized images can mask painful real-world experiences. The work was shown as part of an exhibition (“Speaking Out”) that addresses violence against women, and Humphreys has said she wanted to challenge the notion that life is perfect and highlight how children’s early perceptions of the world differ sharply from reality.

Title: Becoming an Image
Creator: Heather Cassils
Description: A durational performance, photography-based and sculptural piece in which the artist physically attacks a large block of clay in total darkness. The audience and performer are blind in the space; the only light comes from a photographer’s flash, which briefly illuminates the action and “burns” images into viewers’ retinas. Cassils uses trained physical exertion—punches, kicks and movement—to shape the clay over the course of the performance, creating both an abstract sculpture and a series of photographs as documentation of the work. The piece interrogates visibility, the roles of the witness, aggressor and documenter, and discourses around gender and the body, especially within trans and queer contexts, by making the act of seeing itself a central and challenging part of the experience.

Title: The Ninth Wave
Creator: Cai Guo-Qiang
Description: A large-scale installation that reflects on environmental destruction and humanity’s relationship with nature. First exhibited in Shanghai, the work features a life-sized wooden fishing boat overflowing with sculpted animals—such as tigers, pandas, deer, and other creatures—appearing lifeless and washed ashore. The title refers to the “ninth wave,” a term from maritime folklore describing the most powerful and devastating wave in a storm. In the installation, the stranded boat and animals symbolize the aftermath of ecological disaster, evoking themes of pollution, climate change, and collective vulnerability. At the same time, the piece carries a subtle sense of hope, suggesting survival and the possibility of renewal after catastrophe.

Title: Blank Paper
Creator: Yolanda He Yang
Description: A public performance in Times Square in which she silently held up a sheet of blank white paper. The gesture referenced the “Blank Paper” protests that emerged in China against censorship and strict COVID-19 policies. By presenting an empty page—symbolizing both speech and its suppression—He Yang highlighted themes of free expression, surveillance, and solidarity. The stark simplicity of the action invited passersby to reflect on what cannot be openly said, turning absence into a powerful political statement.

Title: Project Dust
Creator: Brother Nut
Description: A performance and environmental art action in Beijing. Over 100 days, he used a vacuum cleaner to collect smog particles from the city’s polluted air. The gathered dust was then compressed and mixed with clay to create a single brick.The project made the invisible threat of air pollution tangible, transforming airborne smog into a solid object. By turning pollution into a brick—a basic building material—Brother Nut highlighted the severity of China’s air quality crisis and invited public reflection on environmental responsibility.

Title: Remembering
Creator: Ai Weiwei
Description: A powerful public art installation created in 2009 to honor the thousands of children who died in the 2008 Sichuan earthquake, a tragedy that was exacerbated by the collapse of poorly constructed schools. The piece consists of 9,000 children's backpacks arranged in a large, sweeping formation. The colorful backpacks are stacked and displayed in a way that represents both the scale of the loss and the haunting memory of the event. The installation was first presented at the Tate Modern in London, and it serves as a stark commentary on government corruption and the systemic failures that led to the tragedy. Also installed in 2009 at the Haus der Kunst in Munich, Germany. The exhibit was part of Ai Weiwei’s broader global effort to raise awareness about human rights and to challenge censorship.

Title: Old People's Home
Creator: Sun Yuan, Peng Yu
Description: A provocative contemporary art installation from 2007 by Chinese artists Sun Yuan and Peng Yu. It consists of 13 hyper-realistic life-size sculptures of frail, elderly figures seated in electric wheelchairs that slowly move around a space and bump into one another. Although at first it may seem like a chaotic retirement-home scene, the figures are actually modeled on well-known political and military leaders (such as Yasser Arafat or Leonid Brezhnev) and evoke familiar world-leader types — complete with drooling, slumped postures, and detailed clothing. The installation functions as a satirical and darkly humorous commentary on global politics: these once-powerful figures are reduced to doddering old age, aimlessly colliding like bumper cars, suggesting critiques of senility, inertia, and the cyclical nature of conflict in world affairs.

Title: Longing for Freedom
Creator: Song Byeok
Description: An art exhibition featuring the work of North Korean defector and artist Song Byeok, showcasing his vivid and provocative paintings that express a deep yearning for personal and political freedom. Originally exhibited in cities like Los Angeles and San Francisco as part of the Human Rights Foundation’s “Art in Protest” program, the show highlighted how Song transformed from a former state propaganda artist—who had once painted idealized images for the North Korean regime—to a creative dissident using art to critique authoritarian oppression and spotlight human rights issues.

Title: Dying to Breathe
Creator: Sim Chi Yin
Description: A deeply human documentary and multimedia work that chronicles the toll of pneumoconiosis (often called “black lung” disease) on Chinese gold miners. Over several years she documented the life of former miner He Quangui, who contracted the irreversible lung disease from years of working without adequate protective gear in small, unregulated mines in rural China. Through intimate portraits, still photographs, and a short film, the project captures his slow physical decline, the emotional impact on his family, and the broader public-health crisis facing millions of workers suffering similar fate. The work aims to put faces to often invisible sufferers, reveal the human costs behind industrial labor, and raise awareness about preventable occupational illness.

Title: Wall of Shame
Creator: Zhang Bingjian
Description: A provocative conceptual portrait installation that confronts political corruption in contemporary China. The series comprise rosy-pink monochrome portraits of Chinese government officials who have been convicted of corruption or graft. Zhang commissions artisans in China’s Dafen village to paint the portraits based on publicly available photos; the uniform pink hue references the color of the 100 yuan note as a symbol of greed. Wall of Shame uses portraiture as a form of social critique.

Title: Dialogue
Creator: Xiao Lu
Description: The installation consists of two telephone booths facing each other, each containing a photograph of a man and a woman, with a mirror and a red telephone inside. During the opening of the China/Avant-Garde Exhibition in Beijing, Xiao Lu fired two gunshots into the installation, shattering the glass. The artist was detained for four days after she turned herself in. Four months later, students demanding a dialogue with the government led the Tiananmen protests and were met with violent suppression. The iconic photograph of an unidentified man blocking a procession of tanks in the square became the global symbol of resistance. The Tiananmen incident resonated with Lu's piece and the work came to be known as the first gunshots of Tiananmen.

Title: My World Is In Your Blind Spot
Creator: Tenzing Ridgol
Description: A conceptual artwork by Tibetan artist Tenzing Rigdol that addresses themes of exile, identity, and political invisibility. In this work, Rigdol reflects on the experience of Tibet and the Tibetan diaspora being overlooked or ignored by the global community. The artist uses flames in Buddha's image tto reflect the self-immolation protest actions of four young men. Through subtle visual strategies and symbolic framing, Rigdol challenges viewers to confront what they fail to see and to question their own position in relation to political conflict and cultural erasure. The work represents a broader political commentary on displacement, perception, and global indifference.

Title: We Stand As One
Creator: Vanilla Chi
Description: The poster was created in response to rising anti-Asian racism and violence and in support ot the Asian community. The design typically features strong, eye-catching typography paired with empowering imagery of Asian identity and solidarity. The poster is available free to download and use in activist causes.

Title: The Wounded Brides
Creator: Li Tingting
Description: A performance-style protest carried out in Beijing in 2012 to raise awareness about domestic violence. On Valentine’s Day that year, Li and two other volunteers walked through a busy commercial street in the Chinese capital wearing white wedding dresses splattered with fake blood, carrying signs and chanting messages such as “Love is not an excuse for violence” to challenge the cultural silence around intimate partner abuse and highlight how common domestic violence is in China.

Title: New Ukraine
Creator: Roti
Description: An unsanctioned public sculpture by French street artist Roti created as a symbolic act of solidarity with Ukrainian protesters during the 2014 Euromaidan uprising in Kyiv’s Independence Square. The roughly 2-metre marble work depicts a woman’s face, hands, and toes emerging from the stone as if rising out of water — a powerful visual metaphor for a nation striving to break free from oppression and turmoil. Currently the scultpure is in the collection of the Maidan museum in Kyiv.

Title: Knitted Dress
Creator: Louise Moerup
Description: The knitted dress made by Louise Moerup for the statue Venus with the Apple in Enghaveparken is a hand-knitted, striped halter dress designed to slip over the nude figure of the classical Venus sculpture. Moerup created the dress as a humorous “knitted intervention” to draw attention to the imbalance in public monuments—many depict clothed historical men, while women are often represented only as nude mythological figures. The act was later shared on social media and helped inspire a wider 2026 movement of Danish knitters clothing statues of nude female figures.

Title: Todos Juntos Podemos Parar el SIDA (Together We Can Stop AIDS)
Creator: Keith Haring
Description: A arge public mural in Barcelona, located in the El Raval neighborhood near the Museu d'Art Contemporani de Barcelona. The mural was created to raise awareness about the AIDS epidemic and encourage collective action against the disease. Painted in Haring’s signature style with bold lines and simplified figures, the red mural shows symbolic images such as a snake labeled “AIDS,” a syringe, human figures cutting or fighting the snake and three human figures in the pose of “see no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil” monkeys visualizing the social stigma around the crisis.

Title: Building Images
Creator: Isabelle Wenzel
Description: A photographic series by Isabelle Wenzel that stages the female body in surreal office environments. In the images, Wenzel contorts her body so that parts of it appear to emerge from desks, cabinets, and other workplace furniture, creating humorous and slightly absurd scenes. The series combines performance, sculpture, and photography, using self-timer shots and repeated poses to capture carefully arranged compositions. By fragmenting the body—showing legs, hips, or torsos interacting with office objects—Wenzel comments on workplace monotony and the stereotypical roles of women, while parodying the way advertising and media often fetishize female body parts.

Title: Ice Watch London
Creator: Olafur Eliasson
Description: A public art installation that raises awareness about climate change. Large blocks of glacial ice from Greenland were placed outside Tate Modern and near Bloomberg’s European headquarters in London. The ice was arranged in a circular formation and left to slowly melt in public, allowing people to touch and experience the ice firsthand. The melting ice acts as a powerful visual reminder of global warming and the rapid loss of Arctic ice.

Title: Melting Men
Creator: Néle Azevedo
Description: A temporary public installation made of hundreds or thousands of small ice figures placed on steps in urban spaces. As the ice sculptures slowly melt in the sun, the work symbolizes human vulnerability, the passage of time, and the effects of climate change.

Title: Naming the Money
Creator: Lubaina Himid
Description: An installation made of life-size painted wooden cut-out figures representing enslaved Africans who were forced to work in European households. Each figure is given a name, voice, and personal story, reclaiming identities that were erased during slavery. The work highlights the hidden histories of slavery in Europe and restores individuality and humanity to the people who were treated as property.

Title: Darfunica
Creator: Nadia Plesner
Description: The painting inspired by Picasso's Guernica re-creates the composition of Picasso’s work but replaces the figures with images referencing the Darfur conflict and modern consumer culture. The painting contrasts scenes of war and human suffering with symbols of luxury brands and celebrity culture, criticizing how media attention often focuses more on fashion and fame than on humanitarian crises. Louis Vuitton sued Plesner for using an image resembling one of its famous handbags, but by 2011 the courts ruled in her favor. The case raised questions about artistic expression, especially when artworks critique consumerism using recognizable luxury brand imagery

Title: Refugee Boat
Creator: Yoko Ono
Description: A participatory installation that draws attention to the global refugee crisis. The work features a life-sized white boat and invites the audience to add their messages of solidarity, concern and peace using blue markers. The space gradually transforms into a sea of blue showcasing different perspectives.

Title: Women's House (Sunglasses)
Creator: Sanja Iveković
Description: A series of posters that combine fashion-style images of women wearing sunglasses with texts describing real stories of domestic violence survivors. The posters were displayed in public spaces in Magdeburg and the localized display in different cities underscore the global issue of violence against women.

Title: I'm Not A Joke
Creator: Daniel Arzola
Description: A series of colorful digital posters created in 2013 that use simple illustrations and bold text to challenge homophobia, bullying, and discrimination against LGBTQ+ people. Each work carries the message I am not a joke, as a response to insults and stereotypes faced by LGBTQ+ individuals thereby emphasizing that their identities should be respected. The series is an example of artivism or art used for activism.

Title: Red Shoes
Creator: Elina Chauvet
Description: A public art installation by Mexican artist Elina Chauvet that has been displayed in places such as Mexico City. The installation consists of hundreds of pairs of women’s shoes painted bright red and arranged in public spaces, often plazas. Each pair symbolizes a woman who has been murdered or has disappeared due to gender-based violence (femicide).

Title: Palas por Pistolas (Shovels for Guns)
Creator: Pedro Reyes
Description: A public art project created in 2008 by Mexican artist Pedro Reyes in Culiacán, Sinaloa, Mexico. The project collected 1,527 guns voluntarily surrendered by citizens, which were then melted down and transformed into shovels. These shovels were used to plant 1,527 trees around the world, symbolically turning instruments of violence into tools for growth and community renewal.

Title: Oráculo Project
Creator: Anonymous
Description: A public intervention in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil by placing red blindfolds on about 100 public statues of historical figures across the city. The red cloth covering the statues’ eyes symbolized shielding these figures from witnessing Brazil’s political corruption and national crisis.

Title: Silueta (Silhouette) series
Creator: Ana Mendieta
Material: Soil, sand, grass, flowers, stones, fire, or blood
Description: A group of performance-based earthworks in which the artist created the outline or impression of her body in natural landscapes. Mendieta shaped these “siluetas” using materials such as soil, sand, grass, flowers, stones, fire, or blood, often documenting them through photographs and film. The works explore themes of identity, exile, femininity, and connection to nature. As a Cuban-born artist living in the United States after being displaced from Cuba as a child, Mendieta used the body-shaped forms embedded in the earth to express a desire to reconnect with the land and with cultural roots.

Title: Teeter-Totter Wall
Creator: Ronald Rael and Virginia San Fratello .
Description: A temporary interactive art and architectural installation created by Ronald Rael and Virginia San Fratello. Installed in July 2019 at the US–Mexico border between El Paso and Ciudad Juárez, the project placed three bright pink seesaws through the steel slats of the border wall so that people on both sides could play together. The installation lasted less than an hour but symbolically transformed the wall from a barrier into a shared point of interaction. Because a seesaw only works when both sides cooperate, the project illustrated how actions on one side of the border affect the other and promoted ideas of connection, empathy, and collaboration between communities.  A powerful commentary on the politics of borders and human connections beyond these constructed divisions.

Title: Families Belong Together
Creator: Andrea Bowers
Description: A politically engaged artwork that uses a bold protest slogan to address immigration and family separation. The piece is made from simple materials like cardboard and LED lights, giving it the look and urgency of a protest sign.

Title: Performing the Torture Playlist
Creator: Adam Harms
Description: A video montage artwork that loops together karaoke-style clips of songs that were reportedly used during interrogations at Guantánamo Bay. Harms demonstrates the transformation of enjoyable popular music into tools of oppression and torture. The piece is part of the conceptual Guantánamo Bay Museum of Art and History, which is the product of the intervention of an international team of artists, curators to remember and memorialize the human rights abuses that occurred in the infamous detention facility.

Title: Ossario (Ossuary)
Creator: Alexandre Orion
Description: An urban art project by Brazilian artist Alexandre Orion in the road tunnels of São Paulo. Using a technique called reverse graffiti, Orion cleaned away layers of soot from vehicle pollution with fabric, creating thousands of skull images on the tunnel walls. The gesture intentionally transformed the tunnel into a kind of “catacomb,” serving as a powerful statement about air pollution, urban neglect, and the potential impact on human health.

Title: Untitled (Billboard)
Creator: Felix Gonzalez-Torres`
Description: A black-and-white photograph of an unmade double bed with two pillows, shown empty after being slept in. The image was displayed on billboards across New York City, presenting an intimate, quiet moment of absence and human presence within a highly public space. The work was created during the height of the AIDS crisis, when LGBTQ+ communities—especially gay men—were facing stigma, neglect, and political inaction. González-Torres lost his partner Ross Laycock to AIDS-related illness, and the empty, rumpled bed quietly references that loss.

Title: The Yanomami Struggle
Creator: Claudia Andujar
Description: A photographic project documenting the lives, culture, and threats faced by the Yanomami people in the Amazon. Combining portraits, environmental imagery, and advocacy, the work highlights their struggle against disease, deforestation, and outside intrusion while calling attention to Indigenous rights. The project developed over decades (beginning in the 1970s) and has been presented in multiple exhibitions internationally.

Title: Burkaphilia
Creator: Behnaz Babazadeh
Description: A multimedia art series (2012) that explores the burqa as a cultural and even fetishized object, challenging how it is perceived—especially in Western contexts. The project uses photography, video, and performance (often featuring the artist herself wearing stylized or unconventional burqas) constructed using American sweets like gummy bears, cotton candy, sour worms, and Fruit Roll-Ups. he use of candy introduces bright, playful, and nostalgic associations, contrasting with the often negative or oppressive image of the burqa in Western media.

Title: Not a Bug Splat
Creator: Artist Collective
Description: A large-scale art installation created in 2014 in the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa region of Pakistan. It consists of a massive portrait of a young girl laid out on the ground so that it is visible from the sky. The project was designed to be seen by drone operators, challenging the military slang “bug splat,” which reduces human targets to indistinct dots on a screen. The work highlights the human cost of drone warfare.

Title: Seven Days of Garbage
Creator: Gregg Segal
Description: A striking visual commentary on modern waste, using portraits of people with their own garbage to highlight how much we consume—and discard—every week. In the series, Segal asked people to collect all their trash for one week and then photographed them lying surrounded by it. The images are carefully staged—often shot from above and set in natural-looking environments—to emphasize how much waste individuals produce. The project makes the invisible problem of everyday garbage visible and personal.

Title: Calligraffiti
Creator: eL Seed
Description: A large-scale mural painted on the Jara mosque’s minaret in the city of Gabès. It blends Arabic calligraphy with graffiti to create a contemporary public artwork rooted in traditional script.The piece features a Quranic verse about unity and mutual understanding, emphasizing respect between different people and cultures.

Title: A Subtlety or The Marvelous Sugar Baby
Creator: Kara Walker
Description: A large-scale installation set in a former sugar refinery in Brooklyn. Its centerpiece was a monumental, sugar-coated sphinx-like figure of a Black woman, surrounded by smaller childlike figures carrying baskets. The work used sugar—a material tied to the history of slavery and colonial labor—to confront viewers with themes of race, exploitation, and the legacy of the transatlantic sugar trade. By combining stereotypical imagery (like the “mammy” figure) with an imposing, sensual form, Walker exposed how Black bodies have been objectified and commodified.

Title: Blued Trees
Creator: Aviva Rahmani
Description: An ongoing ecological artwork by Aviva Rahmani that involves marking trees with bands of blue paint in areas threatened by development, especially pipeline construction.The project operates at the intersection of art, environmental activism, and legal strategy. By framing the marked trees as a continuous, site-specific artwork, Rahmani seeks to protect them under cultural preservation/ copyright laws, effectively using art as a tool to resist deforestation and ecological damage.

Title: We Are the Asteroid
Creator: Justin Brice Guariglia
Material: Text, sandblasted solar-powered LED message board, black rust patina
Description: A conceptual public art installation consisting of a repurposed, solar-powered LED highway sign that displays short, poetic warning messages about climate change. Instead of traffic alerts, the sign flashes aphorisms such as “WARNING: HURRICANE HUMAN” or “WE ARE THE ASTEROID,” reframing environmental crisis as an immediate, human-caused danger. Created in collaboration with philosopher Timothy Morton, the work uses familiar roadside signage to provoke reflection, suggesting that humans themselves are the catastrophic force—like an asteroid—driving ecological destruction.

Title: T.rutt's T.rump Bus
Creator: David Gleeson and Mary Mihelic
Description: A mobile political art installation created from a retired Donald Trump campaign bus that the artists ( cofounders of politically minded art collective T. Rutt,) purchased and transformed into a satirical, anti-Trump artwork. They altered the bus’s branding—changing slogans like “Make America Great Again” to “Make Fruit Punch Great Again” and modifying the name to “T.rump”—and filled it with provocative imagery and messages critiquing Trump’s rhetoric and policies. The artists then drove the bus to rallies and political events across the U.S., where it functioned as a kind of “Trojan horse”:, but ultimately revealing itself as critical commentary.

Title: The Heidelberg Project
Creator: Tyree Guyton
Description: A large-scale outdoor art installation in Detroit that transforms an entire neighborhood into a colorful, immersive environment using found objects and painted structures. Started in 1986 on Heidelberg Street, the project involved covering abandoned houses, lots, and everyday objects—like shoes, clocks, toys, and cars—with bright colors and patterns (especially polka dots). It was both an artistic and social response to urban decay, aiming to draw attention to poverty, neglect, and racial inequality while engaging local residents in creative renewal. The work functions as a community-driven, evolving installation.

Title: Cartoons Against Corruption
Creator: Aseem Trivedi
Description: A cartoon-based campaign launched to protest the rampant corruptio in Indian administration and politics. The artist utilizes provocative imagery, satire and politically charged symbolism to visualize the fallout of corrupt practices. His blunt criticism of the state that employed reinterpretation of national symbols has led to censorship of his website, legal action and sedition charges, sparking debates on free speech.

Title: Kodaikanal Won’t
Creator: Sofia Ashraf
Description: A viral protest rap that uses humor, parody, and sharp lyrics to criticize the environmental damage caused by FMCG company, Hindustan Unilever in Kodaikanal. The performance calls out corporate negligence and  Kodaikanal Won’t combines music, satire, and activism to expose corporate environmental injustice and mobilize public protest.highlights mercury poisoning, worker suffering, and environmental contamination linked to a thermometer factory.

Title: Crocodile Ponds
Creator: Baadal Nanjundaswamy
Description: Baadal Nanjundaswamy is known for turning Bengaluru’s potholes into striking pieces of protest art that blend realism, satire, and civic activism. Instead of just pointing out road damage, he transforms hazards into eye-catching installations to force public attention. One of his most famous works involved converting a large, water-filled pothole into a fake “crocodile pond.” He painted the pothole to resemble murky water and placed a life-sized model crocodile inside it.

Title: Bonded not Bound
Creator: The Fearless Collective
Description: A large participatory mural in Dharavi that serves as a collective statement about agency, belonging, and freedom within dense urban life.. Created with local women, the mural uses the iconic blue tarpaulin color of Dharavi to symbolize physical boundaries in crowded living conditions. It depicts groups of women—often shown in intimate, everyday moments like braiding hair—to represent sisterhood, care, and shared life. The central idea is that while women are bonded through relationships, community, and love, they should not be bound by restrictive social norms or lack of personal space. The artwork highlights the tension between necessary emotional bonds and imposed limitations, affirming the message that connection should be chosen, not enforced.

Title: Nusaybin
Creator: Zehra Doğan
Description: A stark, politically charged depiction of a town devastated by conflict. Based on a real photograph from Nusaybin, it shows ruined buildings and widespread destruction, with Turkish flags hanging over the wreckage—emphasizing the presence of state authority amid the ruins.This painting led to her arrest and imprisonment: Turkish authorities charged Doğan with spreading propaganda, and she was detained in 2017. Her detention drew international attention, and she continued creating art while incarcerated, often using improvised materials. The painting of Nusaybin thus became not only a representation of destruction, but also a symbol of artistic resistance and the risks of political expression.

Title: Journey of a Yellow Man
Creator: Lee Wen
Description: Lee Wen’s Journey of a Yellow Man (1992–2012) is a long-running performance series in which the artist covered his body in bright yellow paint and moved through public spaces. The “Yellow Man” figure confronts racial stereotypes associated with Asian identity, reclaiming the term “yellow” while exposing how it has been used historically in a derogatory way. By placing his painted body in everyday environments, Lee Wen created moments of discomfort and reflection, prompting audiences to confront issues of identity, marginalization, and cultural difference.

Title: Those Without Names
Creator: Carlo Gabuco
Description: A deeply immersive installation shown at a Manila art fair in 2018 that made the violence of the Philippine drug war viscerally real. At its center was an actual armchair where a victim had been killed, marked by a bullet hole, accompanied by a recorded testimony from the victim’s child and a displayed bullet that caused the death. The surrounding space included photographs of killings and mourning, confronting viewers with the scale and human impact of the violence. The work emphasizes how victims of the drug war—thousands killed since Rodrigo Duterte took office—become reduced to anonymous statistics. By using real objects and personal stories, Gabuco transforms abstraction into an emotional, immediate experience, forcing viewers to recognize the humanity behind “those without names.”

Title: Letter from Birmingham City,  Four Little Girls Bombed in a Church
Creator: Faith Ringgold
Description: A poignant serigraph plate from her illustrated edition of Martin Luther King Jr.'s Letter from Birmingham City Jail. The image specifically commemorates the four young African American girls killed in the 1963 church bombing in Birmingham, Alabama, a pivotal, tragic moment in the Civil Rights movement.

Title: Red Sand Project
Creator: Molly Gochman
Description: A participatory public art initiative (started in 2014) that raises awareness about human trafficking and modern-day slavery. In the project, people pour bright red sand into cracks in sidewalks or public spaces. The sand symbolizes vulnerable individuals who “fall through the cracks” of society—those at risk of exploitation but often overlooked. The project was initiated in Miami and gradually spread across all 50 states and around 70 countries with over one million participants.

Title: National AIDS Memorial Quilt
Creator: Cleve Jones and Volunteers
Description: A massive, ongoing community art project that honors people who have died from HIV/AIDS. It was started in 1985 during the height of the AIDS crisis. Each panel of the quilt is typically 3 by 6 feet—the size of a grave—and is handmade by friends, family, or loved ones. Panels often include names, photos, and personal mementos, turning the quilt into both a memorial and a powerful form of activism. Today, the quilt is one of the largest pieces of community folk art in the world, with over 50,000 panels. It has been displayed in locations like the National Mall to raise awareness, reduce stigma, and humanize the impact of the AIDS epidemic.

Title: National AIDS Memorial Grove
Creator: San Francsico Resident Group
Description: A peaceful, landscaped memorial located in Golden Gate Park in San Francisco. It was created to honor the lives lost to HIV/AIDS and to provide a place for reflection, healing, and remembrance. The grove features natural elements like trees, meadows, and walking paths, along with engraved stones and monuments dedicated to individuals and communities affected by the epidemic. Established in the early 1990s and later designated as the nation’s official AIDS memorial, it serves as both a tribute and a living space for education and community gatherings.

Title: The Clothesline Project
Creator: Rachel Carey-Harper and group
Description: A grassroots awareness movement that began in 1990 in Cape Cod. It was created by a group of women as a way to bring attention to violence against women. The project involves survivors (and their loved ones) decorating T-shirts with messages, artwork, or stories about their experiences with violence. These shirts are then hung on a clothesline and displayed publicly. The exhibits communicate personal stories and the projects have spread across 41 states and 5 countries.

Title: Whale Bay, Antartica no. 4
Creator: Zaria Forman
Visualtags: climate change, global warming, melting glaciers
Description: Zaria Forman is known for creating large-scale, hyperrealistic drawings of natural landscapes, especially glaciers, icebergs, and oceans. She uses soft pastels applied by hand to achieve smooth, photographic detail without using paint or brushes. Her work focuses on the beauty and fragility of remote environments, often inspired by travels to places like the Arctic and Antarctica. Through these drawings, Forman raises awareness about climate change, capturing melting ice and shifting ecosystems.

Title: Wasteland Project
Creator: Vik Muniz
Description: Vik Muniz’s Wasteland project (2008–2010) is a socially engaged art initiative in which he collaborated with waste pickers (catadores) at Jardim Gramacho landfill—once one of the world’s largest landfills. Muniz photographed the workers and then, with their help, recreated large-scale portraits of them using recyclable materials found at the site. The project highlights themes of dignity, inequality, and the transformative power of art, and it was documented in the acclaimed film Waste Land.

Title: Pads Against Sexism
Creator: Elonë Kastratia
Description: A form of street art activism that uses menstrual pads as a medium to challenge sexism. The artist placed sanitary pads with handwritten feminist messages in public spaces in Karlsruhe, Germany to challenge taboosand inspire dialogues around sexism and street harassment. By using an object society often finds “uncomfortable,” the project deliberately confronts double standards—where women’s bodies are sexualized but natural processes like periods are shamed. The movement was adopted by students of Kolkata's Jadavpur University in India.

Title: Mr. Prejudice
Creator: Horace Pippin
Description: A powerful painting that confronts racism and injustice in the United States.  In the artwork, a large figure labeled “Mr. Prejudice” dominates the scene, symbolizing hatred and discrimination. He holds a weapon and divides the canvas into two sides: one side shows Black Americans facing violence and oppression (including references to the Ku Klux Klan), while the other shows a more idealized version of America with symbols like the Statue of Liberty.  The work critiques the contradiction between American ideals of freedom and the reality of racial inequality.

Title: Guernica
Creator: Pablo Picasso
Description: A large black-and-white mural that depicts the chaos and suffering caused by the bombing of the Basque town of Guernica during the Spanish Civil War. The painting is filled with distorted, anguished figures—crying women, a wounded horse, and a fallen soldier—symbolizing the pain of innocent civilians caught in violence. Picasso avoids specific details, instead using fragmented forms and stark contrast to convey universal suffering. “Guernica” became a powerful political symbol of resistance, condemning the brutality of war and especially the targeting of civilians. It has since stood as an enduring anti-war image, representing protest against violence, oppression, and injustice worldwide.

Title: The Great Wall of Los Angeles
Creator: Judith F Baca
Description: A massive public mural in Los Angeles that tells the history of California from prehistoric times to the mid-20th century. Stretching for over half a mile along a flood control channel, it is one of the longest murals in the world. The mural was created between the 1970s and 1980s with the help of more than 400 community members, especially local youth, making it a collaborative and community-driven project. The objective is to highlight the experiences of marginalized groups—such as Indigenous peoples, immigrants, and minorities—whose stories are often left out of traditional histories. Through vivid, chronological scenes, it presents a more inclusive and socially conscious narrative of the past. Today the Great Wall functions as a living artwork—still growing, being restored, and updated by new generations to include more recent and diverse histories.

Title: The Strike is Won
Creator: Harry Gottlieb
Description: A social realist artwork that celebrates a labor union victory. It shows a group of workers gathered together in a moment of triumph after successfully winning a strike. The figures are strong, unified, and expressive, emphasizing solidarity and collective power. The artwork conveys a clear message: organized labor can achieve justice and improve working conditions through unity and persistence.

Title: House Beautiful: Bringing the War Home
Creator: Martha Rosler
Description: A series of photomontages begun in the late 1960s during the Vietnam War and revisited in later conflicts. Rosler collages images of war—soldiers, wounded bodies, and destruction—into glossy magazine-style photos of comfortable American homes. By inserting war imagery into familiar interiors, Rosler critiques how conflict is sanitized, ignored, or normalized within affluent societies. The series forces viewers to confront their own complicity in these conflicts.

Title: September
Creator: Gerhard Richter
Description: A small, blurred painting based on photographic images of the September 11 attacks. It depicts the moment when one of the planes strikes the World Trade Center, but the scene is partially obscured by Richter’s signature smudging technique. Rather than presenting a sharp, documentary image, Richter creates emotional distance, encouraging reflection on how such events are remembered and processed. He chose a scale that reflects the visual medium of televisions through which millions received these images and the moment that sensitively renders the undoing of the towers.

Title: Maypole: Take No Prisoners
Creator: Nancy Spero
Material: Handprinting on aluminum, ribbon, steel chain, and aluminum pole with steel base
Description: A large-scale installation that transforms the traditional festive maypole into a stark anti-war monument. A tall central pole is encircled by ribbons and chains from which hang over 100–200 painted aluminum heads, many depicting severed, screaming, or grotesquely expressive faces. Drawing on imagery from her Vietnam-era War Series, the installation connects past and present conflicts—particularly the Vietnam and Iraq wars—highlighting the cyclical, ongoing nature of violence and political oppression. Installed at the entrance of the Italian Pavilion, it confronts viewers immediately, forcing a physical and emotional encounter with the brutality of war.
